stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres Fire Surges to 89,000 Acres, Now Colorado's Seventh Largest Wildfire on Record; New Fremont County Evacuations Ordered
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres Fire southwest of Pueblo grew to 89,055 acres by Sunday, making it the seventh largest wildfire on record in Colorado, while containment reached 14% by Sunday afternoon with about 1,100 personnel deployed. A damage assessment team in Custer County confirmed no fatalities in the areas where the fire originated near the Aspen Acres campground, offering some relief after more than 180 structures were destroyed.

On Sunday evening, Fremont County Sheriff issued a mandatory evacuation order for neighborhoods along Colorado Highway 67, including communities such as Coal Creek, Rockvale and Williamsburg, as the fire made a 2,000-acre surge during the day. Authorities said the fire, which started June 29, was human-caused, but no further details on its origin have been released.

A separate fire, the 3,000-acre Willow Fire burning southwest of Turquoise Lake near Leadville, prompted new evacuation orders Sunday for residents along County Roads 11 and 11A and the Lake Fork Trailer Park. The Willow Fire remained at 0% containment as of Sunday.

Firefighters reported more favorable conditions with no red flag warnings in effect, shifting tactics from large tanker drops to precision helicopter operations. Seven helicopters are on scene with an eighth en route from California, with crews focusing on protecting the communities of Rye and Colorado City.

stories.headline: Man Arrested on Suspicion of Murder After Denver Stabbing Kills One Woman, Injures Two Others
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested Robert Tafoya, 40, on suspicion of first-degree murder after a stabbing in the 1300 block of Lowell Boulevard in the West Colfax neighborhood Saturday afternoon killed one woman and injured two others. Officers responded to the scene at approximately 2:24 p.m., and paramedics transported three victims to the hospital, where one woman later died.

Tafoya was booked into Denver's Downtown Detention Center on charges of first-degree murder, attempted first-degree murder, and first-degree assault, according to inmate records. Police said Tafoya and the victims were known to each other, though the nature of their relationship was not disclosed.

The stabbing occurred near West 13th and West 14th avenues, close to the Lakewood Gulch area. The victim will be identified by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner.

stories.headline: Denver Officer Shoots and Injures Man Armed With Knife Following Harassment Call
stories.summary:
A Denver police officer shot and injured a man Sunday after responding to a harassment complaint near East 51st Avenue and Washington Street at approximately 11:40 a.m. Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas said two officers found the man holding a long kitchen-style knife roughly 12 inches in length and gave repeated commands to drop the weapon.

The man initially fled from officers, stopped once, and then fled again before turning toward officers near East 50th Avenue and Washington Street. At that point, one officer fired, striking the man multiple times. Paramedics transported the man to a hospital; his condition was not immediately available Sunday afternoon.

Chief Thomas described the incident as a recurring situation involving the same individual, noting police had received prior reports of the person harassing people in the area while armed, but had not previously made contact with him. No officers were injured. The identities of the officer and the man shot were not publicly released.

stories.headline: Colorado Gazette Columnists Analyze Primary Surprises: Weiser, Kiros Victories Signal Voter Frustration With Washington Insiders
stories.summary:
Denver Gazette columnists Cronin and Loevy offered analysis Sunday of Colorado's primary results, arguing that anti-Washington sentiment drove the defeats of longtime incumbents including U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet in the governor's race and 30-year Congresswoman Diana DeGette, who lost to 29-year-old Democratic Socialist Melat Kiros in Denver's congressional district. The columnists wrote that Bennet's negative attacks on Attorney General Phil Weiser backfired, calling them out of character and too aggressive, while Weiser ran a disciplined campaign positioning himself as more forcefully anti-Trump.

On the Republican side, the analysis noted that Victor Marx, founder of All Things Possible Ministries in Colorado Springs and a former Marine, leads the GOP gubernatorial nomination despite having no elected office experience and opposition from the state's Republican establishment. The columnists flagged that Marx has a "colorful and complex personal history" that will face scrutiny going forward.

The column also noted that U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper won renomination but with a narrower margin than expected against a left-leaning challenger despite an 8-to-1 fundraising advantage, suggesting broader voter dissatisfaction with incumbents across the political spectrum.

stories.headline: Nuggets in Trade Discussions With Lakers Over Center Jonas Valanciunas Ahead of July 8 Guarantee Deadline
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets are in trade talks with the Los Angeles Lakers regarding veteran center Jonas Valanciunas, according to reporting from The Stein Line cited by Heavy.com. The Nuggets have had Valanciunas available in trade conversations since Denver's first-round playoff exit, and the Lakers' interest is described as welcome by the organization.

The situation carries urgency due to a July 8 deadline, after which only $2 million of Valanciunas' salary remains guaranteed if he is waived, compared to a $10 million obligation for the 2026-27 season if he remains on the roster. The Nuggets also explored various deals during the NBA Draft but did not move Valanciunas or their No. 26 overall pick at that time.

Valanciunas, 34, played 32 games for Denver after being acquired from the Washington Wizards during the 2024-25 season. The Lakers are also reportedly considering Kevon Looney as an alternative big man option, but interest in Valanciunas is described as "only growing."

stories.headline: Avalanche Sign Defenseman Christian Wolanin to One-Year Contract for 2026-27 Season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Sunday the signing of defenseman Christian Wolanin to a one-year contract for the 2026-27 season. Wolanin, 31, spent the 2025-26 season with the Providence Bruins in the AHL, recording 31 points in 53 games and finishing among league defensemen in multiple categories including power-play goals and game-winning markers.

Wolanin has appeared in 86 career NHL games with the Ottawa Senators, Los Angeles Kings, Buffalo Sabres and Vancouver Canucks, posting 23 points. His best NHL season came in 2018-19 with Ottawa, when he set career highs with 30 games played and 12 points.

The signing carries a family connection to Colorado: Wolanin's father, Craig, played 364 games with the Avalanche and Quebec Nordiques over his 13-year NHL career and was a member of the 1996 Stanley Cup championship team. Christian Wolanin was born in Quebec City and is a left-shot defenseman standing 6-foot-2, 190 pounds.

stories.headline: Broncos Count on Defensive Lineman Sai'vion Jones to Step Up in 2026 Following John Franklin-Myers' Departure
stories.summary:
With veteran defensive end John Franklin-Myers having departed to the Tennessee Titans, the Denver Broncos are looking to second-year lineman Sai'vion Jones as a high-upside option to fill the void entering the 2026 season. Jones, 23, was a third-round pick in the 2025 NFL Draft out of LSU, and head coach Sean Payton indicated the team may use a rotation of players rather than naming a single replacement.

Jones stands 6-foot-5 and 289 pounds, and while his rookie year showed promise, he has yet to establish himself as a consistent presence in the Broncos' defensive line rotation. Other candidates include reserve Eyioma Uwazurike, third-round 2026 rookie Tyler Onyedim, and veteran Malcolm Roach.

Franklin-Myers had formed a productive partnership with All-Pro Zach Allen that was central to the Broncos' defensive success. The defensive line depth chart is expected to be a key competition when training camp opens.

stories.headline: Colorado Nursing Homes Face Worsening Staffing Crisis as Complaints Hit 20 Per Day and Severe Deficiency Rate Reaches 40%
stories.summary:
A Denver Post investigation finds that 40% of Colorado nursing homes had severe deficiencies — defined as problems that can threaten residents' lives — in 2025, up sharply from 14% in 2019. Only four states recorded higher deficiency rates, and formal complaints from residents of Colorado's 210 nursing homes and 675 assisted living centers reached 7,426 in fiscal year 2025, a rate of roughly 20 complaints per day.

Colorado's Long-Term Care Ombudsman director Leah McMahon described conditions in many facilities as leading to "loss and grief, no joy, no animation, no engagement in life" and a complete erosion of dignity. The approximately 38,069 residents of these facilities often receive as little as two hours of direct care per day, and federal requirements such as 24/7 registered nurse coverage have been relaxed.

Industry leaders attribute the crisis to an inability to retain skilled caregivers at prevailing wage levels, compounded by a demographic shift toward an older population. The trend is expected to intensify as Colorado's senior population continues to grow, putting additional pressure on an already strained system.

stories.headline: Supercharged Bubbles Threaten Glen Canyon Dam as Lake Powell Drops to Historic Lows, Federal Officials Warn
stories.summary:
Federal officials are confronting a growing threat to Glen Canyon Dam as Lake Powell's water level falls to historic lows, creating conditions in which air bubbles implode with destructive force inside the dam's tubes and turbines — the same phenomenon that nearly caused catastrophic failure during record-high 1983 flooding. Bureau of Reclamation reports indicate the dam could be forced to halt hydropower generation before year's end to avoid severe damage.

The bureau has spent millions adding protective layers to water release valves, and state and federal officials are engaged in high-stakes negotiations over how to manage the dam's limitations. After a record-poor winter snowpack, officials are weighing how to maintain adequate water levels while fulfilling downstream obligations to cities including Phoenix, Las Vegas and Los Angeles, as well as agricultural users.

The situation has direct implications for Colorado, which contributes the majority of the Colorado River's flow through mountain snowpack and is a party to negotiations over the river's management. Emergency water releases have already been made to maintain minimum operational levels at the dam.

stories.headline: Wildlife Study Finds Billionaire's 8-Foot Fence at San Luis Valley Ranch Harms 30 Species; Owner Rejects Negotiations
stories.summary:
A joint wildlife study commissioned by the owner of the 83,000-acre Cielo Vista Ranch and Costilla County commissioners found that an 8-foot perimeter fence around the San Luis Valley property is potentially detrimental to at least 30 species, including federally listed Canada lynx and silverspot butterflies, as well as elk, eagles, deer, cutthroat trout and northern leopard frogs. The 83-page report recommends numerous modifications, including adding wildlife openings, raising the fence bottom 16 inches off the ground, enlarging wire-grid squares and removing barbed wire.

The study concluded that a 5-foot fence would be sufficient to contain the ranch's bison herd. Costilla County commissioners recently made the report public after it had been held confidential since its completion last fall; the report was part of a court-ordered mediation.

Ranch owner William Harrison's legal team informed the county there would be "no negotiations involving the contents of the report," according to county officials. Local residents organized as the La Sierra Environmental Guardian Committee called the fence an "ill-conceived" project and said Harrison's refusal to comply debunks his stated justification of bison containment. The land battle involves longstanding claims by local communities tied to the Sangre de Cristo Land Grant of 1844.

stories.headline: Colorado Film Commissioner Details Strategy for Turning Sundance Relocation Into Sustainable Industry
stories.summary:
Lauren Sloan, six months into her role as Colorado's film, television and media commissioner, outlined the state's strategy for converting the momentum around Sundance Film Festival's move to Boulder into a lasting economic driver in a Colorado Sun interview. Sundance signed a 10-year contract with Boulder in March 2025 following a multi-year courting effort that included a $34 million state tax credit and $1.5 million for the Boulder coalition.

Sloan said the festival's January 2026 debut in Boulder represents a rare spotlight for Colorado's film and media industry, offering an opportunity to attract filmmakers and productions to the state. The Colorado Office for Film, Television and Media has been building toward this moment through a series of expanded tax incentives, including a four-year tax incentive signed into law and a major sales tax rebate program.

Colorado faces competition from states with more established incentive structures, including Utah at 25%, New Mexico at 30% and Georgia with larger credits. Sloan said being proactive and "friendly" to productions is as important as financial incentives, and that the office's core mission is to promote production as an economic and jobs driver across the state.

stories.headline: Seven Paddleboarding Destinations Highlighted Across Colorado as Summer Recreation Season Peaks
stories.summary:
The Denver Gazette has compiled a guide to seven paddleboarding destinations across Colorado, ranging from Aurora Reservoir near the metro area to Grand Lake, Shadow Mountain Lake and Dillon Reservoir in Summit County. The guide notes that paddleboarding is among the most accessible water activities in Colorado given that swimming is prohibited in most of the state's bodies of water.

The guide includes locations accessible to Denver metro residents such as Aurora Reservoir, which offers rentals from Memorial Day through Labor Day, and Bear Creek Lake Park in Lakewood. More distant destinations include Lake San Cristobal near Lake City, Lemon Reservoir near Durango and Spinney Mountain Reservoir near Woodland Park.

The Gazette included a safety note emphasizing that paddleboarders without life jackets die every year in Colorado, and that wearing a Coast Guard-approved life jacket significantly reduces risk. Windy conditions were flagged as a particular hazard that can leave paddleboarders stranded or pushed into dangerous areas.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I look like I was ordered off the internet by someone who has never seen a human being before.

topStory.headline: House Speaker Johnson Vows Legislative Action on Birthright Citizenship After Supreme Court Rebuffs Trump Order
topStory.summary:
House Speaker Mike Johnson said Sunday that Congress must pursue legislation to restrict birthright citizenship following a Supreme Court ruling last week that struck down President Donald Trump's executive order seeking to redefine who qualifies as an American citizen. Speaking on Fox News Sunday, Johnson said Republicans are "looking at all angles" and pledged to advance a bill "immediately" if a viable legislative path exists, while acknowledging that a constitutional amendment would take considerably longer.

The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Trump v. Barbara, with Chief Justice John Roberts writing for the majority that children born in the United States satisfy the citizenship clause of the 14th Amendment regardless of their parents' immigration status. Roberts wrote that the framers of the 14th Amendment extended citizenship to "every free-born person in this land" and that the court was keeping that promise. Justice Clarence Thomas, whose dissent Johnson praised on Sunday, argued the majority had made a "terrible error" by extending citizenship to children of foreign temporary visitors and people in the country illegally.

Following the ruling, Trump urged lawmakers on social media to begin work on legislation "TODAY," claiming no constitutional amendment was necessary to achieve the change through statute. Justice Brett Kavanaugh, in a concurring opinion, indicated Congress may have authority to establish exceptions to birthright citizenship for children born to parents present in the country unlawfully, which could provide a legal foundation for a legislative approach. Sen. Lindsey Graham of South Carolina and Rep. Brian Babin of Texas had already introduced legislation in the previous Congress that sought to narrow the definition of persons considered "subject to the jurisdiction" of the United States under the 14th Amendment.

The scope of so-called birth tourism — the practice of traveling to the United States specifically to give birth and obtain citizenship for a child — is a point of dispute. Estimates from researchers have ranged from approximately 2,000 to 39,000 such births annually, and a group of 140 university professors told the Supreme Court that the practice accounts for an "infinitesimal proportion" of U.S. births. Democrats have characterized birth tourism as a marginal issue, while Republican leaders including Sen. Ted Cruz have called it a blatant abuse of immigration law.

Amending the Constitution to restrict birthright citizenship would require approval from two-thirds of both chambers of Congress and ratification by three-fourths of state legislatures, a threshold considered extremely difficult to reach. The push for a legislative fix, by contrast, would face its own constitutional challenges given the Supreme Court's reaffirmation of the 14th Amendment's citizenship clause, setting up a prolonged legal and political battle heading into the 2026 midterm elections.
